reasoning:
Although the UK launched a world-first targeted gonorrhea vaccination program in August 2025 (using the Bexsero/4CMenB cross-protective vaccine), the resolution of this question depends exclusively on whether the "Vaccine(s)" column is updated for Gonorrhea on the Wikipedia page "List of infectious diseases". 

A direct fetch of that Wikipedia page on the knowledge cutoff date (November 9, 2025) reveals that the table has not been edited since October 1, 2025. More notably, the "Vaccine(s)" column appears systematically unmaintained and is left completely blank for virtually all infectious diseases listed—even for those with globally ubiquitous vaccines such as Hepatitis A/B, Influenza, Cholera, and Diphtheria.

Because the entire column is neglected, the natural probability of a spontaneous, isolated update occurring specifically for Gonorrhea is extremely low. Even if a forecaster attempts to edit the page to resolve the question as "Yes", such an isolated edit to a totally blank column is highly conspicuous and liable to be reverted by Wikipedia administrators for consistency. As a result, the probability starts near the minimum allowed value for the shortest time horizon. 

Given the rule that uncertainty increases over time, the probabilities grow incrementally for the more distant dates, representing the cumulative but still slim chance that either Wikipedia editors undertake a massive project to populate the entire vaccine column, or the ongoing UK rollout generates enough sustained buzz to prompt a lasting anomalous edit.
